answer:
**Manu’s *Manusmṛti* Chapter 4 (starting at Mn_4.1) extensively outlines the duties, livelihoods, and vows of the householder (*gṛhastha*), with a focus from Mn_4.13 onwards on the *snātaka* (Brahmin Vedic graduate returning to lay life after studentship).**[^1] **It lists ~150 behavioral rules covering daily rites, purity, diet, travel, speech, Vedic study restrictions (*anadhyāyas*), and social etiquette (e.g., honoring guests at Mn_4.29–31, avoiding contempt at Mn_4.135–36).**[^2]

**Viṣṇu Smṛti lacks a parallel chapter dedicated to the *snātaka*’s life.** Its 100 chapters cover dharma topics diffusely (e.g., witnesses in ch.8, purification in ch.22, penances in chs.46–56, conduct in ch.71, self-control in ch.72). “Vedic graduate”/*snātaka* appears sparingly: ch.54.29 equates violation of *snātaka* vows to major sins requiring fasting;[^3] **ch.63.49 lists “Vedic graduate” among superiors (with king, woman, etc.) to whom one yields the path.**[^4] **No comprehensive *snātaka* code parallels Manu ch.4.** Chs.71–72 (proper conduct, self-control) echo Manu’s ethical tone but apply generally, not *snātaka*-specifically.[^5]

**Without a Viṣṇu parallel, systematic rule-by-rule comparison (e.g., on “meeting a stranger”) is impossible.** Manu has no explicit “stranger” rule but covers encounters via guest-honoring (Mn_4.29: no guest unhonored; Mn_4.30–31: honor Vedic experts, avoid heretics) and road etiquette (cf. Manu ch.2.138–39: prioritize *snātaka* over king when meeting).[^6] **Viṣṇu ch.63.49 similarly mandates yielding path to “Vedic graduate”/king/etc., aligning (not opposing) Manu.**[^7] **No Viṣṇu rule forbids what Manu permits.** 

**The corpus cannot fully answer as assumed: no Viṣṇu *snātaka* chapter exists for topic-specific opposition analysis.** For general conduct parallels, see Manu ch.4 vs. Viṣṇu chs.71–72 (e.g., both praise self-control/good conduct for longevity/prosperity, Mn_4.156–58 ~ Vi_71.91–93).[^8]
